summary: Documented Shell Icons registry mapping for the shortcut-arrow overlay. Microsoft guidance shows that Shell Icons value `29` can be pointed at `%windir%\System32\shell32.dll,-50` to remove shortcut arrows, and removing the custom Shell Icons value restores the default overlay behavior. The app writes that exact value.

setting.name: Shortcut Arrow Overlay
setting.casual_explanation: Windows normally shows a small arrow on shortcut icons. This setting swaps that overlay resource so the arrow is no longer shown.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change it because they dislike the shortcut arrow and want a cleaner desktop or Explorer look.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Removing the arrow makes shortcuts look cleaner, but it also removes the visual hint that an icon is a shortcut instead of the real file or app.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft support documents the Explorer Shell Icons mapping and states that value `29` can reference `%windir%\System32\shell32.dll,-50` to remove shortcut arrows. The same Microsoft guidance states that deleting the custom Shell Icons value restores the default Windows arrow overlay.

allowed_values.label: Windows default arrow
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses its default shortcut-arrow overlay because no custom Shell Icons value is forcing an alternate icon.

allowed_values.label: Shortcut arrow removed
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses the documented alternate icon resource for Shell Icons value 29, removing the normal shortcut arrow overlay.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes Shell Icons value 29 = %windir%\System32\shell32.dll,-50, which matches the Microsoft support guidance for removing shortcut arrows.

windows_defaults.label: Windows default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows Explorer shortcut overlay behavior without a custom Shell Icons override

states.rationale: Microsoft support says removing the custom Shell Icons value restores the default shortcut-arrow behavior.

windows_defaults.notes: This record tracks the Shell Icons override only, not every possible third-party icon-overlay customization.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Anyone who wants shortcuts to stay visually obvious
avoid_for: Users who strongly dislike the shortcut arrow

states.rationale: Keep the default Explorer shortcut overlay by not forcing a Shell Icons override.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its normal shortcut indicator.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a cleaner-looking icon set without arrows.
tradeoffs: The default Explorer visual style is preserved.

recommended_profiles.label: No shortcut arrow
intended_for: Users who care about desktop aesthetics
People who intentionally want the overlay removed
avoid_for: Shared or support-heavy systems where distinguishing shortcuts matters

states.rationale: Use the Microsoft-documented Shell Icons value that removes the shortcut arrow.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want a cleaner icon appearance and already know which icons are shortcuts.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You rely on the arrow as an important visual cue.
tradeoffs: The shortcut-vs-real-item distinction becomes less obvious.

evidence.title: Microsoft Q&A: remove shortcut arrow using Shell Icons value 29
evidence.summary: A Microsoft External Staff moderator documents creating Explorer\Shell Icons, adding string value 29, and setting it to %windir%\System32\shell32.dll,-50 for the shortcut-arrow override.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes Shell Icons value 29 = %windir%\System32\shell32.dll,-50 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft guidance shows creating `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Icons`, adding string value `29`, and setting it to `%windir%\System32\shell32.dll,-50` for the no-arrow shortcut overlay. The app writes that exact Shell Icons override.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer; create key Shell Icons; create String Value 29; set it to %windir%\System32\shell32.dll,-50.
validation_proof.notes: The cited Microsoft Q&A answer is product guidance from a Microsoft External Staff moderator rather than an ADMX or policy page, so this record should be treated as medium-strength proof.
